text:
# Page 1^page=1
Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition, Chapter 10: File-System Interface

# Page 2^page=2
10.2 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Chapter 10: File-System Interface u File Concept u Access Methods u Directory Structure u File-System Mounting u File Sharing u Protection

# Page 3^page=3
10.3 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Objectives u To explain the function of file systems u To describe the interfaces to file systems u To discuss file-system design tradeoffs, including access methods, file sharing, file locking, and directory structures u To explore file-system protection

# Page 4^page=4
10.4 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 4 File Systems u 3 criteria for long-term information storage: p Should be able to store very large amount of information p Information must survive the processes using it p Should provide concurrent access to multiple processes u Solution: p Store information on disks in units called files p Files are persistent, and only owner can explicitly delete it p Files are managed by the OS u File Systems: How the OS manages files!

# Page 5^page=5
10.5 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 5 File System uFile System: Layer of OS that transforms block interface of disks (or other block devices) into Files, Directories, etc. uFile System Components pDisk Management: collecting disk blocks into files pNaming: Interface to find files by name, not by blocks pProtection: Layers to keep data secure pReliability/Durability (持久) : Keeping of files durable despite crashes, media failures, attacks, etc

# Page 6^page=6
10.6 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 6 File System u User vs. System View of a File p User’s view: 4 Durable Data Structures p System’s view (system call interface): 4 Collection of Bytes (UNIX) 4 Doesn’t matter to system what kind of data structures you want to store on disk! p System’s view (inside OS): 4 Collection of blocks (a block is a logical transfer unit, while a sector is the physical transfer unit) 4 Block size ³ sector size; in UNIX, block size is 4KB

# Page 7^page=7
10.7 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 7 Translating from User to System View u What happens if user says: give me bytes 2—12? p Fetch block corresponding to those bytes p Return just the correct portion of the block u What about: write bytes 2—12? p Fetch block p Modify portion p Write out Block u Everything inside File System is in whole size blocks（块为单位） p For example, getc(), putc() Þ buffers something like 4096 bytes, even if interface is one byte at a time u From now on, file is a collection of blocks (i.e. systems view inside OS) File System

# Page 8^page=8
10.8 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Concept u Contiguous logical address space u Types: p Data 4numeric 4character 4binary p Program

# Page 9^page=9
10.9 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Structure u None - sequence of words, bytes u Simple record structure p Lines p Fixed length p Variable length u Complex Structures p Formatted document p Relocatable load file u Can simulate last two with first method by inserting appropriate control characters（可以对方法1增加控制符实现后两种,如下表） u Who decides: p Operating system p Program

# Page 10^page=10
10.10 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 10 File Structure n Byte Sequence: unstructured, most commonly used(a) n Record sequence: r/w in records, used earlier(b) n Complex structures, e.g. tree(c) - Data stored in variable length records; location decided by OS

# Page 11^page=11
10.11 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Attributes p Name – only information kept in human-readable form p Identifier – unique tag (number) identifies file within file system p Type – needed for systems that support different types p Location – pointer to file location on device p Size – current file size p Protection – controls who can do reading, writing, executing p Time, date, and user identification – data for protection, security, and usage monitoring p Information about files are kept in the directory structure, which is maintained on the disk

# Page 12^page=12
10.12 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations u File is an abstract data type u Basic file operations p Create p Write p Read p Reposition within file p Delete p Truncate (删除内容,保留文件目录)

# Page 13^page=13
10.13 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations u Create p Allocates disk space p Makes an entry in the file directory u Delete p Searches the directory p Releases all file space p Erases the directory entry for the file

# Page 14^page=14
10.14 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations p Read u requires specifying file name and the memory location where the next block of the file to be put u Searches the directory to find the file’s location u Reads the block of the file into memory u Updates the read pointer

# Page 15^page=15
10.15 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations u Write p requires specifying file name and the information to be written to the file p Searches the directory to find the file’s location p Writes to the file according to the write pointer to the location in the file p Updates the writer pointer

# Page 16^page=16
10.16 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations u Repositioning within a file p Known as a file seek p Searches the directory to find the file’s entry p Updates the current-file-position pointer to a given value u Turncate p Erases the contents of a file but keeps its attributes ü Only the file length be reset to 0 p Releases its file space

# Page 17^page=17
10.17 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Operations u Open(Fi) :make a file ready for reading/writing. p searches the directory structure on disk for entry Fi p moves the content of entry to memory u Close (Fi) : mark the completion of operation on file. p moves the content of entry Fi in memory to directory structure on disk

# Page 18^page=18
10.18 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open Files u Most of the file operations involve searching the directory for the entry of the named file. To avoid this constant searching, many OS require that an open() system call be made before a file first used actively. u The OS keeps a small table, called the open-file table, containing information about all open files. u The open() takes a file name and searches the directory, copying the directory entry into the open-file table. u The open() system call typically returns a pointer to the entry in the open-file table.

# Page 19^page=19
10.19 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open Files u There may be several processes opening the same file at the same time. u OS uses two levels of internal tables: p Process(or User) open-file table 4 Tracks all files opened by a process 4 Stores information about the use of the file, e.g. current file pointer, access rights and accounting information. 4 Each entry in process open-file table in turn points to a system- wide open-file table. p System open-file table 4 Contains process-independent information, such as the disk location of the file, access date, file size and open count.

# Page 20^page=20
10.20 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open Files  Process open-file table/System open-file table

# Page 21^page=21
10.21 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open Files u Several pieces of data are needed to manage open files: p File pointer: pointer to last read/write location, per process that has the file open; p Access rights: per-process access mode information p File-open count: counter of number of times a file is open – to allow removal of data from open-file table when last processes closes it; p Disk location of the file: the information to locate the file on the disk;

# Page 22^page=22
10.22 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open File Locking u File locks are useful for files that are shared by several processes which provided by some operating systems and file systems u File locks allow one process to lock a file and prevent other processes from gaining access to it. u File locks provide functionality similar to reader-writer locks. p Shared lock: like reader lock, allowing reading currently p Exclusive lock: like writer lock, only one process can acquire at a time.

# Page 23^page=23
10.23 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Open File Locking  Further more ,OS may provide either mandatory or advisory file-locking mechanisms. p Mandatory(强制锁) – access is denied depending on locks held and requested p Advisory(建议锁) – processes can find status of locks and decide what to do

# Page 24^page=24
10.24 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Locking Example – Java API import java.io.*; import java.nio.channels.*; public class LockingExample { public static final boolean EXCLUSIVE = false; public static final boolean SHARED = true; public static void main(String arsg[]) throws IOException { FileLock sharedLock = null; FileLock exclusiveLock = null; try { RandomAccessFile raf = new RandomAccessFile("file.txt", "rw"); // get the channel for the file FileChannel ch = raf.getChannel(); // this locks the first half of the file - exclusive exclusiveLock = ch.lock(0, raf.length()/2, EXCLUSIVE); /** Now modify the data . . . */ // release the lock exclusiveLock.release();

# Page 25^page=25
10.25 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Locking Example – Java API (cont) // this locks the second half of the file - shared sharedLock = ch.lock(raf.length()/2+1, raf.length(), SHARED); /** Now read the data . . . */ // release the lock sharedLock.release(); } catch (java.io.IOException ioe) { System.err.println(ioe); }finally { if (exclusiveLock != null) exclusiveLock.release(); if (sharedLock != null) sharedLock.release(); } } }

# Page 26^page=26
10.26 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 26 File Naming u Motivation: Files abstract information stored on disk Ø You do not need to remember block, sector, … Ø We have human readable names u How does it work? Ø Process creates a file, and gives it a name 4 Other processes can access the file by that name Ø Naming conventions are OS dependent 4 Usually names as long as 255 characters is allowed 4 Digits and special characters are sometimes allowed 4 MS-DOS and Windows are not case sensitive, UNIX family is

# Page 27^page=27
10.27 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 27 File Extensions u Name divided into 2 parts, second part is the extension u On UNIX, extensions are not enforced by OS Ø However C compiler might insist on its extensions 4These extensions are very useful for C u Windows attaches meaning to extensions Ø Tries to associate applications to file extensions

# Page 28^page=28
10.28 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Types – Name, Extension  Two major types of files:  Program files 4 Source code 4 Object code 4 Executable program  Data files 4 Character or ASCII file 4 Binary file 4 Free-formatted text file 4 Formatted or structured file  There are different subtypes of files.  They are often indicated by the file extension.

# Page 29^page=29
10.29 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 29 File System Patterns  How do users access files? u Sequential Access 4bytes read in order (“give me the next X bytes, then give me next, etc”) u Random Access 4read/write element out of middle of array (“give me bytes i—j”)

# Page 30^page=30
10.30 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Access Methods  Sequential Access read next write next reset no read after last write(rewrite)  Direct Access read n write n position to n read next write next rewrite n n = relative block number

# Page 31^page=31
10.31 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Simulation of Sequential Access on Direct-access File

# Page 32^page=32
10.32 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Example of Index and Relative Files  Other access methods can be built on top of a direct-access method.  These methods generally involve the construction of an index for the file.  存取方法与索引结构有直接关系

# Page 33^page=33
10.33 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Directory Structure F 1 F 2 F 3 F 4 F n Directory Files u Both the directory structure and the files reside on disk u Backups of these two structures are kept on tapes u Some systems store millions of file on disk, to manage all these data, we need to organize them. This organization involves the use of directory. A directory is a collection of nodes containing information about all files.

# Page 34^page=34
10.34 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Disk Structure u Disk can be subdivided into partitions，Partitions also known as minidisks, slices; u Disks or partitions can be RAID protected against failure; u Disk or partition can be used raw – without a file system, or formatted with a file system; u Entity containing file system known as a volume; u Each volume containing file system also tracks that file system’s info in device directory or volume table of contents; *RAID(Redundant Array of Independent Disks)

# Page 35^page=35
10.35 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition A Typical File-system Organization  A disk may be divided into many parts. p A part may hold an individual file system. This is called a partition.  Sometimes, several disks are combined together to hold one large file system. p This collection of disks is also called a partition

# Page 36^page=36
10.36 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Operations Performed on Directory u Search for a file u Create a file u Delete a file u List a directory u Rename a file u Traverse the file system

# Page 37^page=37
10.37 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Organize the Directory (Logically) to Obtain u Efficiency – locating a file quickly u Naming – convenient to users p Two users can have same name for different files p The same file can have several different names u Grouping – logical grouping of files by properties, (e.g., all Java programs, all games, …)

# Page 38^page=38
10.38 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Single-Level Directory u A single directory for all users pNaming problem pGrouping problem

# Page 39^page=39
10.39 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Two-Level Directory u Separate directory for each user u Path name u Can have the same file name for different user u Efficient searching u No grouping capability

# Page 40^page=40
10.40 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Tree-Structured Directories u Efficient searching u Grouping Capability u Current directory (working directory) ü cd /spell/mail/prog ü type list

# Page 41^page=41
10.41 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Tree-Structured Directories (Cont) Absolute or relative path name u Creating a new file is done in current directory u Delete a file rm <file-name> u Creating a new subdirectory is done in current directory mkdir <dir-name> Example: if in current directory /mail mkdir count mail prog copy prt exp count Deleting “mail” Þ deleting the entire subtree rooted by “mail”

# Page 42^page=42
10.42 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Path Names A UNIX directory tree

# Page 43^page=43
10.43 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Acyclic-Graph Directories (无循环) u Have shared subdirectories and files

# Page 44^page=44
10.44 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Acyclic-Graph Directories (Cont.) u An acyclic-graph directory structure is more flexible than a simple tree structure, but it is also more complex u Two different names (aliasing) u Several problems: p A file may have multiple absolute path names. p File deletion: when can the space allocated to a shared file be deallocated and reused?

# Page 45^page=45
10.45 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Acyclic-Graph Directories (Cont.) u If dict deletes list Þ dangling pointer Solutions: 1.Keep backpointers of links for each file(保持后备指针); 2.Leave the link, and delete only when accessed later(断开链接); 3.Keep reference count of each file(引用计数); u New directory entry type Ø Link – another name (pointer) to an existing file Ø Resolve the link – follow pointer to locate the file list

# Page 46^page=46
10.46 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition General Graph Directory  How do we guarantee no cycles? u Allow only links to file not subdirectories u Every time a new link is added use a cycle detection algorithm to determine whether it is OK u Garbage collection is necessary only because of possible cycles in graph. extremely time consuming

# Page 47^page=47
10.47 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File System Mounting  A file system must be mounted before it can be accessed  How to mount? ① The OS is given the name of the device and the mount point—the location within the directory structure where the file system is to be attached. u Typically, a mount point is an empty directory. ② Then, the OS verifies that the device contains a valid file system; ③ Finally, the OS notes in its directory structure that a file system is mounted at the specified mount point.

# Page 48^page=48
10.48 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition (a) Existing. (b) Unmounted Partition A unmounted file system (i.e. Fig. (b)) is mounted at a mount point（最右图）

# Page 49^page=49
10.49 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 49 File System Mounting u Mount allows two FSes to be merged into one For example you insert your floppy into the root FS mount(“/dev/fd0”, “/mnt”, 0)

# Page 50^page=50
10.50 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing u Sharing of files on multi-user systems is desirable； u Sharing may be done through a protection scheme； u On distributed systems, files may be shared across a network； u Network File System (NFS) is a common distributed file- sharing method；

# Page 51^page=51
10.51 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing – Multiple Users u User IDs identify users, allowing permissions and protections to be per- user； u Group IDs allow users to be in groups, permitting group access rights； u Uses networking to allow file system access between systems p Manually via programs like FTP p Automatically, seamlessly using distributed file systems p Semi automatically via the world wide web File Sharing – Remote File Systems

# Page 52^page=52
10.52 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing – Remote File Systems u Client-server model allows clients to mount remote file systems from servers p Server can serve multiple clients p Client and user-on-client identification is insecure or complicated p NFS is standard UNIX client-server file sharing protocol p CIFS (Common Internet File System) is standard Windows protocol p Standard operating system file calls are translated into remote calls u Distributed Information Systems (distributed naming services) such as LDAP, DNS, NIS, Active Directory implement unified access to information needed for remote computing

# Page 53^page=53
10.53 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing – Remote File Systems The basic NFS architecture for UNIX systems.

# Page 54^page=54
10.54 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing u Storage Area Network (SAN) is a current technology to provide large storage capacities to a large user population. p It is a high-speed network dedicated to the task of transporting data for storage and retrieval. p It connects together computers and storage devices to allow sharing of the pool of storage devices. p It is adopted by large institutions.

# Page 55^page=55
10.55 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing u Cloud storage is the newest technology to host data in a collection of nodes over the cloud. p Service is usually provided by third party, usually data center. p Common storage: iCloud, dropbox, Google drive.

# Page 56^page=56
10.56 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing – Failure Modes u Remote file systems add new failure modes, due to network failure, server failure u Recovery from failure can involve state information about status of each remote request u Stateless protocols such as NFS include all information in each request, allowing easy recovery but less security

# Page 57^page=57
10.57 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition File Sharing – Consistency Semantics u Consistency semantics specify how multiple users are to access a shared file simultaneously u Similar to Ch 7 process synchronization algorithms 4 Tend to be less complex due to disk I/O and network latency (for remote file systems） u Andrew File System (AFS) implemented complex remote file sharing semantics 4 AFS has session semantics, Writes only visible to sessions starting after the file is closed u Unix file system (UFS) implements: 4 Writes to an open file visible immediately to other users of the same open file 4 Sharing file pointer to allow multiple users to read and write concurrently

# Page 58^page=58
10.58 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Protection u File owner/creator should be able to control: p what can be done p by whom u Types of access p Read p Write p Execute p Append p Delete p List

# Page 59^page=59
10.59 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Access Lists and Groups u Mode of access: read, write, execute u Three classes of users RWX a) owner access 7 Þ 1 1 1 RWX b) group access 6 Þ 1 1 0 RWX c) public access 1 Þ 0 0 1 u Ask manager to create a group (unique name), say G, and add some users to the group. u For a particular file (say game) or subdirectory, define an appropriate access. owner group public chmod 761 game Attach a group to a file chgrp G game

# Page 60^page=60
10.60 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Windows XP Access-control List Management

# Page 61^page=61
10.61 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition A Sample UNIX Directory Listing

# Page 62^page=62
10.62 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 1. 10.9 2. 10.11 assignment

# Page 63^page=63
Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition, End of Chapter 10
